message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T105 (ledgerRefs goals:G6)
Branch: implement/T105    Base commit: 8d8fa77 (current main HEAD)
headline: Config surface: per-path bind mode with optional global default (I5 config)
description: Per Q42: add `bind = "source"|"device"|"auto"` to each [[paths]] block (internal/config/config.go Path struct) plus an optional top-level global default; per-path overrides global; default is `auto` (today's selectDeviceBinds behavior). config.validate rejects unknown values and normalizes empty to the global/auto default. Plain TOML — no versioning cost. This task is CONFIG SURFACE ONLY — parse/validate/normalize; do NOT rewire selectDeviceBinds consumption (that is a later task). Keep the parsed value available on the Path struct for a future consumer.
acceptance: internal/config/config_test.go covers: default auto when omitted, per-path override beats global, unknown value fails fast at load with a message naming the path. go test ./... green.

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes. Read internal/config/config.go for the existing Path struct, the normalize()/validate() idioms, and how selectDeviceBinds currently derives auto behavior (so your default matches it).

If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t105-fix implement/T105` in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

52	// SchedulerPolicy selects the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind
53	// runs. It is a bounded enum: the P1 active-backup failover (default) or the T21
54	// weighted-aggregation policy. The policy is an explicit config choice — active-
55	// backup stays selectable so the P1 behaviour is never removed, only extended.
56	type SchedulerPolicy string
57	
58	const (
59		// PolicyActiveBackup is the P1 single-active-path failover scheduler: all egress
60		// on the highest-priority live path, every other path idle (data-thrift by
61		// construction). It is the default when [scheduler] is omitted.
62		PolicyActiveBackup SchedulerPolicy = "active-backup"
63		// PolicyWeighted is the T21 weighted-aggregation scheduler: under load a single
64		// flow is striped across both paths in proportion to per-path RTT/loss-derived
65		// weight, collapsing to the primary at low load (5G stays ~idle) with hysteresis,
66		// and per-path send-pacing to bound bufferbloat.
67		PolicyWeighted SchedulerPolicy = "weighted"
68	)
69	
70	func (p SchedulerPolicy) valid() bool {
71		return p == PolicyActiveBackup || p == PolicyWeighted
72	}
73	
74	// Weighted-aggregation policy defaults (T21). They are applied when [scheduler]
75	// selects the weighted policy but leaves a knob at its zero value, so a minimal
76	// `policy = "weighted"` block is usable without hand-tuning every threshold. They
77	// are conservative bring-up values; the empirical per-path pace is sized from a
78	// measured BDP in a bandwidth-capped fixture (P0 findings §7, deferred to T35/T23),
79	// so the shipped default leaves pacing DISABLED and only bounds distribution.
80	const (
81		// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity, in frame-selection
82		// slots per second, that the aggregation load-gate compares offered load against
83		// AND (when pacing is enabled) the per-path token-bucket refill rate. It is a
84		// synthetic proxy for path capacity: there is no measured BDP (P0 §7), so capacity
85		// is expressed in the only unit the scheduler sees — Pick() invocations per second.
86		defaultPerPathCapacityFPS = 10000.0
87		// defaultEngageFraction engages aggregation once offered load exceeds this
88		// fraction of one path's capacity — i.e. a single path is nearly saturated.
89		defaultEngageFraction = 0.9
90		// defaultDisengageFraction collapses back to primary-only once offered load falls
91		// below this fraction of one path's capacity. It is strictly below the engage
92		// fraction: the gap is the hysteresis band that stops the 5G path from dribbling
93		// on/off around a single threshold (requirement 2, data-thrift).
94		defaultDisengageFraction = 0.5
95		// defaultCollapseDwell is how long offered load must stay continuously below the
96		// disengage fraction before aggregation collapses to primary-only — the temporal
97		// half of the hysteresis, mirroring the active-backup failback dwell so a brief
98		// lull does not repeatedly park then re-engage the metered path.
99		defaultCollapseDwell = 2 * time.Second
100		// defaultLoadTau is the time constant of the exponentially-weighted offered-load
101		// rate estimator. It smooths the instantaneous Pick() arrival rate so the gate
102		// reacts to sustained load, not single-frame bursts.
103		defaultLoadTau = 200 * time.Millisecond
104		// defaultPacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst (in frame slots)
105		// when pacing is enabled: the bucket admits up to this many frames instantaneously
106		// before the refill rate binds, absorbing normal jitter without inflating a queue.
107		defaultPacingBurstFrames = 64.0
108		// defaultWeightRTTFloor floors the RTT in the weight formula so a path reporting a
109		// near-zero RTT (cold estimator, no samples yet) cannot be handed unbounded weight.
110		defaultWeightRTTFloor = 1 * time.Millisecond
111		// defaultWeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root so a zero-loss
112		// path gets a large-but-finite weight and two zero-loss paths split by inverse-RTT
113		// alone rather than both diverging.
114		defaultWeightLossFloor = 1e-3
115	)
116	
117	// SchedulerConfig selects and tunes the send scheduler. When the [scheduler] block
118	// is omitted the policy defaults to active-backup and every weighted knob is
119	// ignored, so existing configs keep the P1 behaviour unchanged. The weighted knobs
120	// are validated (and defaulted) only when the weighted policy is selected.
121	type SchedulerConfig struct {
122		// Policy selects the scheduler; defaults to active-backup when empty.
123		Policy SchedulerPolicy `toml:"policy"`
124	
125		// PerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity in frame-selection slots
126		// per second: the denominator the aggregation load-gate compares offered load
127		// against, and the per-path pacing refill rate when PacingEnabled. Must be > 0
128		// under the weighted policy.
129		PerPathCapacityFPS float64 `toml:"per_path_capacity_fps"`
130		// EngageFraction engages aggregation when offered load exceeds
131		// EngageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS. Must be in (0,1].
132		EngageFraction float64 `toml:"engage_fraction"`
133		// DisengageFraction collapses aggregation to primary-only once offered load stays
134		// below DisengageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS for CollapseDwell. Must be in
135		// [0,EngageFraction) — strictly below EngageFraction so the two form a hysteresis
136		// band.
137		DisengageFraction float64 `toml:"disengage_fraction"`
138		// CollapseDwell is the sustained-low-load dwell before collapsing to primary-only
139		// (hysteresis). Must be >= 0.
140		CollapseDwell time.Duration `toml:"collapse_dwell"`
141		// LoadTau is the offered-load rate estimator's time constant. Must be > 0.
142		LoadTau time.Duration `toml:"load_tau"`
143	
144		// PacingEnabled turns per-path send-pacing on. When false the token buckets are
145		// bypassed (a documented no-op — P0 §7 could not empirically size the pace in the
146		// unmetered fixture), but PerPathCapacityFPS still drives the aggregation gate.
147		PacingEnabled bool `toml:"pacing_enabled"`
148		// PacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst in frame slots. Must be > 0
149		// when PacingEnabled.
150		PacingBurstFrames float64 `toml:"pacing_burst_frames"`
151	
152		// WeightRTTFloor floors RTT in the weight formula (must be > 0 under weighted).
153		WeightRTTFloor time.Duration `toml:"weight_rtt_floor"`
154		// WeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root (must be > 0 under
155		// weighted).
156		WeightLossFloor float64 `toml:"weight_loss_floor"`
157	}

159	// BDPSizing is a per-path pacing sizing derived from a MEASURED path, in the
160	// frame-selection-slot units the weighted scheduler pace uses (defect D22). It is the
161	// empirical alternative to the synthetic frame-count defaults: CapacityFPS replaces
162	// PerPathCapacityFPS and BurstFrames replaces PacingBurstFrames.
163	type BDPSizing struct {
164		// CapacityFPS is the sustained per-path frame rate the measured bottleneck bandwidth
165		// supports (the token-bucket refill rate and aggregation-gate denominator).
166		CapacityFPS float64
167		// BurstFrames is the bandwidth-delay product expressed in frames (bandwidth x RTT,
168		// i.e. one RTT of in-flight frames): the token-bucket burst that absorbs one RTT of
169		// jitter without building a standing queue.
170		BurstFrames float64
171	}
172	
173	// defaultAvgWireFrameBytes is the conservative average on-wire outer-frame size used
174	// when deriving the per-path pace from an operator-declared bandwidth at config load
175	// (T53): a full IPv4 path MTU, since a full-MTU DATA datagram occupies about one path
176	// MTU on the wire. It mirrors bind.DefaultPathMTU (1500) rather than importing it —
177	// internal/bind imports internal/config, so config cannot import bind without a cycle
178	// (the same mirror-with-cross-reference pattern as maxFECDeadline above). Sizing
179	// capacity with the full-MTU frame is the conservative floor: smaller average frames
180	// would yield a HIGHER frame rate, so this never over-paces a path.
181	const defaultAvgWireFrameBytes = 1500.0
182	
183	// SizePacingFromBDP derives the weighted scheduler's per-path pacing parameters from a
184	// measured path instead of the synthetic frame-count default (defect D22). The shipped
185	// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS (10000, ~115 Mbit/s at full MTU) sits far above a realistic
186	// slow uplink, so the aggregation gate may never engage and — with pacing enabled — the
187	// pace never binds; deriving capacity from the measured bottleneck bandwidth fixes both.
188	//
189	// capacity_fps = bandwidth / (8 * avg wire frame bytes) — frames/s the link sustains.
190	// burst_frames = capacity_fps * RTT — one RTT of in-flight frames (equivalently
191	// BDP_bytes / avg wire frame bytes, since the bandwidth-delay product BDP = bandwidth * RTT).
192	//
193	// bandwidthBitsPerSec is the measured bottleneck bandwidth (bits/s), rtt the measured
194	// path RTT, and avgWireFrameBytes the average on-wire outer-frame size (a full-MTU
195	// datagram plus frame.DataOverhead is the conservative choice). It fails fast on a
196	// non-positive input rather than emitting a nonsensical (zero/negative/Inf) sizing.
197	func SizePacingFromBDP(bandwidthBitsPerSec float64, rtt time.Duration, avgWireFrameBytes float64) (BDPSizing, error) {
198		if bandwidthBitsPerSec <= 0 {
199			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing bandwidth must be > 0 bit/s, got %g", bandwidthBitsPerSec)
200		}
201		if rtt <= 0 {
202			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing RTT must be > 0, got %s", rtt)
203		}
204		if avgWireFrameBytes <= 0 {
205			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing average wire frame size must be > 0 bytes, got %g", avgWireFrameBytes)
206		}
207		const bitsPerByte = 8.0
208		capacityFPS := bandwidthBitsPerSec / (bitsPerByte * avgWireFrameBytes)
209		burstFrames := capacityFPS * rtt.Seconds()
210		return BDPSizing{CapacityFPS: capacityFPS, BurstFrames: burstFrames}, nil
211	}
212	
213	// bandwidthUnit is a recognised link_bandwidth suffix and its bit/s multiplier.
214	type bandwidthUnit struct {
215		suffix string
216		mult   float64
217	}
218	
219	// bandwidthUnits are the accepted operator-facing bandwidth suffixes, longest-first so
220	// "gbit" is matched before "bit". SI decimal multipliers (k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9) over a
221	// bit/s base; "bps" is accepted as an alias of "bit" (and "kbps"/"mbps"/"gbps" likewise).
222	var bandwidthUnits = []bandwidthUnit{
223		{"gbit", 1e9}, {"gbps", 1e9},
224		{"mbit", 1e6}, {"mbps", 1e6},
225		{"kbit", 1e3}, {"kbps", 1e3},
226		{"bit", 1}, {"bps", 1},
227	}
228	
229	// parseBandwidth parses an operator-declared link bandwidth such as "50Mbit", "1Gbit",
230	// or "500kbit" into bits per second. It requires an explicit bit/s unit suffix so a
231	// bare unitless number cannot be silently misread, and fails fast on an empty or
232	// otherwise unparseable value — a mistyped bandwidth is rejected at config load.
233	func parseBandwidth(s string) (float64, error) {
234		lower := strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(s))
235		if lower == "" {
236			return 0, errors.New("empty bandwidth")
237		}
238		for _, u := range bandwidthUnits {
239			if !strings.HasSuffix(lower, u.suffix) {
240				continue
241			}
242			num := strings.TrimSpace(lower[:len(lower)-len(u.suffix)])
243			val, err := strconv.ParseFloat(num, 64)
244			if err != nil {
245				return 0, fmt.Errorf("invalid number %q", num)
246			}
247			return val * u.mult, nil
248		}
249		return 0, fmt.Errorf("missing bit/s unit suffix (want e.g. %q)", "50Mbit")
250	}
251	
252	// FEC group-close deadline default (T24). Applied only when [fec] is enabled but
253	// leaves the deadline at its zero value, so a minimal `enabled = true` block emits
254	// parity for a partially-filled group promptly under low load rather than stranding
255	// it until the size threshold fills.
256	const defaultFECDeadline = 5 * time.Millisecond
257	
258	// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor mirrors adaptivefec.DefaultSafetyFactor (1.5), the
259	// simulation-proven controller default, restated here (with this cross-reference rather
260	// than an import) so a minimal `adaptive = true` block gets the proven tuning without
261	// coupling config to the controller package. NOTE: 1.5 sizes M for the mean loss with
262	// modest variance headroom; a tight residual SLA under a specific loss/geometry may need a
263	// higher factor (e.g. at 5% loss with K=10 the 1.5 default sizes M=1, ~1% residual — raise
264	// this to lift M and drive the residual down).
265	const defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor = 1.5
266	
267	// maxFECDeadline bounds the FEC group-close deadline at load time (T24, defect #4). It
268	// MUST stay at or below the multipath Bind's authoritative bound (bind.maxFECDeadline =
269	// resequencerTimeout/2 = 125ms): a group flushed by the deadline emits its parity
270	// `deadline` after opening, and the reconstructed frames must reach the receive
271	// resequencer BEFORE it skips the gap (its 250ms per-gap timeout) — otherwise recovery
272	// is structurally too late (the gap is skipped, the recovered frame dropped as past the
273	// release point) while /metrics would still count it reconstructed. Rejecting an
274	// over-large deadline at load makes that coupling fail-fast and explicit rather than
275	// silently defeating FEC. Kept in lockstep with bind.maxFECDeadline (the packages
276	// cannot import each other, so the value is mirrored with this cross-reference).
277	const maxFECDeadline = 125 * time.Millisecond
278	
279	// maxFECShards mirrors the Reed-Solomon field limit enforced in internal/fec: a
280	// coding group carries at most 256 shards (data + parity) total over GF(2^8). It
281	// is restated here so config load fails fast on an over-large ratio at the right
282	// locus, with the same bound the codec enforces internally.
283	const maxFECShards = 256
284	
285	// FEC configures the fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon forward-error-correction plane (T24).
286	// It is DISABLED by default (like pacing), so an existing config with no [fec] block
287	// runs the datapath exactly as before — FEC transparent, no parity on the wire. When
288	// enabled, each group of DataShards (K) inner datagrams is protected by ParityShards
289	// (M) parity frames, letting the receiver reconstruct up to M lost data frames per
290	// group at a fixed M/K parity overhead.
291	type FEC struct {
292		// Enabled turns the FEC plane on. When false every other field is ignored and the
293		// datapath carries no parity.
294		Enabled bool `toml:"enabled"`
295		// DataShards is K: the number of inner datagrams grouped before parity is emitted.
296		// Must be >= 1 when enabled.
297		DataShards int `toml:"data_shards"`
298		// ParityShards is M: the parity frames emitted per group and the maximum number of
299		// per-group data losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1 when enabled. In
300		// adaptive mode it is the CEILING (and the receiver's decoder cardinality) — the
301		// controller drives the per-group parity in [0,ParityShards] to track measured loss.
302		ParityShards int `toml:"parity_shards"`
303		// Deadline bounds grouping latency: a partially-filled group is flushed (parity
304		// emitted over its current data frames) once this much time has elapsed since its
305		// first frame. Defaults to defaultFECDeadline when enabled and left zero; must be
306		// > 0 after defaulting.
307		Deadline time.Duration `toml:"deadline"`
308		// Adaptive opts the send-side FEC into the closed-loop controller (T27/T29): the
309		// per-group parity count tracks the measured per-path loss instead of standing at
310		// the fixed ParityShards ratio, so a clean path spends near-zero overhead while a
311		// lossy one is masked. It is OFF by default, so an existing [fec] block keeps the
312		// fixed-ratio behaviour (T24) byte-for-byte. When true, ParityShards is reinterpreted
313		// as the controller's parity ceiling (see above); the receiver is unchanged because a
314		// group coded with fewer parity shards decodes against the ParityShards-ceiling codec
315		// unchanged (klauspost's parity is prefix-consistent).
316		Adaptive bool `toml:"adaptive"`
317		// SafetyFactor is the adaptive controller's headroom multiplier over the measured mean
318		// loss the per-group parity is sized to mask (adaptivefec.Config.SafetyFactor). It is
319		// the residual-loss LEVER: the controller sizes M so M/(K+M) >= SafetyFactor*loss, so a
320		// higher factor spends more parity to keep the post-recovery residual under a tighter
321		// bound against binomial per-group variance. Applies only in adaptive mode; defaults to
322		// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor (the simulation-proven controller default) when left zero
323		// AND target_residual is unset, and must be >= 1. Ignored (and must stay zero) in fixed
324		// mode, and mutually exclusive with target_residual (set one, not both).
325		SafetyFactor float64 `toml:"safety_factor"`
326		// TargetResidual is the adaptive controller's target POST-RECOVERY residual-loss SLA
327		// (a fraction in (0,1)) and the PRIMARY adaptive sizing surface (D26/T46): the
328		// controller derives the per-group parity M by inverting the binomial residual model
329		// E[max(0,D-M)]/K (D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss)) to the smallest M meeting this target,
330		// capped at the parity ceiling (parity_shards). It SUPERSEDES safety_factor — set
331		// EITHER a residual SLA (target_residual, recommended: it maps an operator's loss
332		// budget directly to redundancy) OR the bare headroom multiplier (safety_factor), never
333		// both. Applies only in adaptive mode; unset (0) selects the safety_factor path. Must
334		// be in (0,1) when set. Ignored (and must stay zero) in fixed mode.
335		TargetResidual float64 `toml:"target_residual"`
336	}
337	
338	// applyDefaults fills the group-close deadline when FEC is enabled and the deadline
339	// was left at zero. It is a no-op for a disabled block, so a config that never turns
340	// FEC on keeps an empty [fec] surface.
341	func (f *FEC) applyDefaults() {
342		if !f.Enabled {
343			return
344		}
345		if f.Deadline == 0 {
346			f.Deadline = defaultFECDeadline
347		}
348		// The SafetyFactor default is applied only in the legacy sizing mode: when
349		// target_residual is set it is the primary surface and safety_factor stays inert
350		// (0), so defaulting it would spuriously trip the mutual-exclusion check.
351		if f.Adaptive && f.TargetResidual == 0 && f.SafetyFactor == 0 {
352			f.SafetyFactor = defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor
353		}
354	}
355	
356	// validate enforces the FEC ratio invariants, failing fast so a mis-tuned parity
357	// ratio is rejected at load rather than panicking the codec at runtime. A disabled
358	// block needs no tuning. The bounds mirror internal/fec's own Config.validate.
359	func (f FEC) validate() error {
360		if !f.Enabled {
361			if f.Adaptive {
362				return errors.New("fec.adaptive = true requires fec.enabled = true (adaptive FEC is meaningless with the plane off)")
363			}
364			return nil
365		}
366		if f.DataShards < 1 {
367			return fmt.Errorf("fec.data_shards must be >= 1 when FEC is enabled, got %d", f.DataShards)
368		}
369		if f.ParityShards < 1 {
370			return fmt.Errorf("fec.parity_shards must be >= 1 when FEC is enabled, got %d", f.ParityShards)
371		}
372		if f.DataShards+f.ParityShards > maxFECShards {
373			return fmt.Errorf("fec.data_shards + fec.parity_shards must be <= %d (Reed-Solomon field limit), got %d", maxFECShards, f.DataShards+f.ParityShards)
374		}
375		if f.Deadline <= 0 {
376			return fmt.Errorf("fec.deadline must be > 0 when FEC is enabled, got %s", f.Deadline)
377		}
378		if f.Deadline > maxFECDeadline {
379			return fmt.Errorf("fec.deadline must be <= %s (safely below the receive resequencer's per-gap timeout so deadline-flushed recovery lands before the gap is skipped), got %s", maxFECDeadline, f.Deadline)
380		}
381		if f.Adaptive {
382			if f.TargetResidual != 0 {
383				// Residual-SLA sizing mode (primary): target_residual governs and safety_factor
384				// stays inert, so setting both is an ambiguous mis-config — reject it. NaN is
385				// caught explicitly because every ordered comparison against it is false.
386				if math.IsNaN(f.TargetResidual) || f.TargetResidual <= 0 || f.TargetResidual >= 1 {
387					return fmt.Errorf("fec.target_residual must be a finite value in (0,1), got %g", f.TargetResidual)
388				}
389				if f.SafetyFactor != 0 {
390					return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor and fec.target_residual are mutually exclusive; target_residual is the primary residual-SLA surface — set one, not both")
391				}
392			} else if f.SafetyFactor < 1 {
393				// SafetyFactor is defaulted to defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor when zero, so a value
394				// below 1 here is an explicit mis-set: the controller requires >= 1 (masking less
395				// than the mean loss is nonsensical), matching adaptivefec.Config.Validate.
396				return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor must be >= 1 in adaptive mode, got %g", f.SafetyFactor)
397			}
398		} else if f.SafetyFactor != 0 || f.TargetResidual != 0 {
399			return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor / fec.target_residual are only meaningful in adaptive mode (set fec.adaptive = true), got safety_factor=%g target_residual=%g", f.SafetyFactor, f.TargetResidual)
400		}
401		return nil
402	}

404	// Path is one physical WAN uplink. The edge binds each path's UDP socket to
405	// SourceAddr so the upstream router pins it to the intended WAN; the
406	// concentrator learns the real per-path endpoints from authenticated traffic.
407	type Path struct {
408		// Name is a stable human-readable identifier for the path (e.g. "starlink").
409		Name string `toml:"name"`
410		// SourceAddr is the local source IP the path's socket binds to.
411		SourceAddr netip.Addr `toml:"-"`
412		// SourceAddrRaw is the TOML string form of SourceAddr; parsed in normalize.
413		SourceAddrRaw string `toml:"source_addr"`
414		// DestAddr is the OPTIONAL per-path concentrator endpoint ("ip:port") the
415		// edge sends this path's datagrams to. It is unset on the concentrator (which
416		// learns edge endpoints from traffic) and optional on the edge: when a single
417		// public concentrator IP fronts all uplinks (the common source-routed case)
418		// the peer's wireguard endpoint is reused for every path; when the paths reach
419		// the concentrator on distinct addresses, set dest_addr per path.
420		DestAddr netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
421		// DestAddrRaw is the TOML string form of DestAddr; parsed in normalize.
422		DestAddrRaw string `toml:"dest_addr"`
423		// LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec is the OPERATOR-DECLARED bottleneck bandwidth of this
424		// uplink in bits/s, parsed from LinkBandwidthRaw in normalize. It is used ONLY to
425		// size the weighted scheduler's per-path pace from the bandwidth-delay product
426		// (SizePacingFromBDP) when the weighted policy runs with pacing ENABLED (T53, Q20);
427		// it is OPERATOR-DECLARED, not runtime-measured — wanbond never auto-tunes it live.
428		// Zero means "not declared" (the synthetic default pace is kept).
429		LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec float64 `toml:"-"`
430		// LinkBandwidthRaw is the TOML string form of the declared bandwidth, e.g.
431		// "50Mbit" / "1Gbit" / "500kbit" (SI bit/s units; the "bit" suffix may be written
432		// "bps"). Parsed in normalize; a non-positive or unparseable value fails fast.
433		LinkBandwidthRaw string `toml:"link_bandwidth"`
434		// LinkRTT is the OPERATOR-DECLARED baseline RTT of this uplink, parsed from
435		// LinkRTTRaw in normalize. It is the delay term of the bandwidth-delay-product pace
436		// burst (one RTT of in-flight frames); required (> 0) when LinkBandwidth is set and
437		// pacing is enabled under the weighted policy, ignored otherwise.
438		LinkRTT time.Duration `toml:"-"`
439		// LinkRTTRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of LinkRTT, e.g. "45ms". Parsed in
440		// normalize; an unparseable or non-positive value fails fast.
441		LinkRTTRaw string `toml:"link_rtt"`
442	}

452	// Peer is one WireGuard peer.
453	type Peer struct {
454		PublicKey Key `toml:"public_key"`
455		// Endpoint is the peer's SINGLE tunnel address (edge -> concentrator); the
456		// legacy single-endpoint config form, mutually exclusive with EndpointsRaw.
457		// Empty on the concentrator, which roams the edge's endpoint dynamically.
458		// A bare `endpoint = "..."` is normalized in resolveEndpoints to a one-
459		// element Endpoints list, so it stays behavior-identical to the pre-T54
460		// config surface.
461		Endpoint string `toml:"endpoint"`
462		// EndpointsRaw is the ORDERED list of concentrator (peer) endpoint address:port
463		// strings for the edge-side hub-failover config surface (Q18): index 0 is the
464		// active/primary concentrator, the rest are ordered standbys. Edge-only; a
465		// concentrator declaring it is a config error (validate). Mutually exclusive
466		// with the legacy Endpoint field.
467		EndpointsRaw []string `toml:"endpoints"`
468		// Endpoints is EndpointsRaw (or the single legacy Endpoint, as a one-element
469		// list) parsed to netip.AddrPort and de-duplicated; populated by
470		// resolveEndpoints in normalize(). This is the shape T57's hub-failover
471		// switch consumes: Endpoints[0] is the active concentrator, Endpoints[1:]
472		// are the ordered standbys to fail over to in order when all paths to the
473		// active one are down. A hostname entry (Q35) contributes NO element here at
474		// load time — resolution happens at runtime, not at config load (Q30) — so
475		// Endpoints only ever holds the literal-address entries, in order.
476		Endpoints []netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
477		// DNS is the explicit per-peer opt-in (Q29) for hostname endpoint entries: a
478		// hostname entry in endpoint/endpoints without dns = true is a config load
479		// error naming this flag. Default-off, so an existing IP-literal config never
480		// takes a new code path. Edge-only; a concentrator declaring dns = true is a
481		// config error, mirroring the endpoints-not-meaningful-for-concentrator rule.
482		DNS bool `toml:"dns"`
483		// EndpointSpecs is the ordered, typed per-entry parse of the endpoint/
484		// endpoints entries (Q35): each entry is either a literal address:port
485		// (IsName=false, Addr set — parsed with EXACTLY today's netip.ParseAddrPort
486		// path) or, when DNS opts in, a hostname:port (IsName=true, Host/Port set).
487		// Populated by resolveEndpoints in normalize(); order matches the TOML list.
488		EndpointSpecs []EndpointSpec `toml:"-"`
489		// AllowedIPs are the CIDR ranges routed to this peer.
490		AllowedIPs []string `toml:"allowed_ips"`
491		// PSK is this peer's per-peer override of the outer-control PSK (G4
492		// multi-peer concentrator groundwork). With a single configured peer it
493		// must be left UNSET: the top-level Config.PSK remains the single-peer
494		// default, so a legacy single-peer config carrying only the top-level
495		// `psk` parses and behaves byte-identically to before this field existed.
496		// With more than one peer, validate requires it to be present and
497		// pairwise-distinct across peers (T81, Q21) — the top-level psk alone
498		// cannot discriminate which peer authenticated an inbound frame, and equal
499		// per-peer psks would defeat that authenticated demux. No datapath code
500		// path consumes PSK yet; it is parsed, validated, and exposed only.
501		PSK Key `toml:"psk"`
502		// Name is this peer's human-readable identifier (G4 multi-peer
503		// concentrator groundwork), analogous to Path.Name. Unused and optional
504		// with a single peer; required and must be unique across peers when more
505		// than one is configured (T81, Q21). Not yet consumed by any datapath code
506		// path.
507		Name string `toml:"name"`
508	}

510	// EndpointSpec is one parsed peer endpoint entry (Q35). A literal address:port
511	// entry parses via netip.ParseAddrPort and sets Addr (IsName=false); a
512	// hostname:port entry (accepted only when the owning Peer opts in with
513	// dns = true) sets Host/Port and IsName=true instead — no resolution happens at
514	// config load (Q30 defers it to runtime).
515	type EndpointSpec struct {
516		// Host is the hostname, set only when IsName is true.
517		Host string
518		// Port is the port for a hostname entry, set only when IsName is true.
519		Port uint16
520		// Addr is the parsed literal address:port, set only when IsName is false.
521		Addr netip.AddrPort
522		// IsName reports whether this entry is a hostname (true) or an IP literal
523		// (false).
524		IsName bool
525	}
526	
527	// resolveEndpoints canonicalizes the peer's endpoint config into the ordered
528	// Endpoints list (Q18) and the ordered, typed EndpointSpecs list (Q35): the
529	// legacy single Endpoint field and the new ordered EndpointsRaw list are
530	// mutually exclusive input forms, but a bare `endpoint = "..."` is normalized
531	// here to a ONE-ELEMENT list — so it stays behavior-identical to the pre-T54
532	// single-defaultRemote config surface. Each entry is tried FIRST as a literal
533	// netip.AddrPort ("host:port"), the same format bind.Multipath.ParseEndpoint
534	// requires of the UAPI endpoint string at runtime — an all-literal config
535	// takes EXACTLY this path, unchanged, with the same errors and the same
536	// duplicate detection as before T67 (Q29). Only when that parse fails, AND the
537	// host portion is not itself a malformed IP literal, is the entry split as
538	// host:port and treated as a hostname, gated behind the peer's explicit DNS
539	// opt-in (Q29); a hostname entry without the opt-in is a config error naming
540	// the flag. No resolution happens here (Q30 defers it to runtime). Duplicates
541	// are rejected within each of the two namespaces: a literal duplicating a
542	// literal, or a hostname:port duplicating another hostname:port.
543	func (p *Peer) resolveEndpoints() error {
544		if p.Endpoint != "" && len(p.EndpointsRaw) > 0 {
545			return errors.New("endpoint and endpoints are mutually exclusive; endpoint is the single-entry legacy form of endpoints")
546		}
547		raw := p.EndpointsRaw
548		if p.Endpoint != "" {
549			raw = []string{p.Endpoint}
550		}
551		seen := make(map[netip.AddrPort]struct{}, len(raw))
552		seenNames := make(map[string]struct{}, len(raw))
553		endpoints := make([]netip.AddrPort, 0, len(raw))
554		specs := make([]EndpointSpec, 0, len(raw))
555		for _, s := range raw {
556			ap, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(s)
557			if err == nil {
558				if _, dup := seen[ap]; dup {
559					return fmt.Errorf("duplicate endpoint %q", s)
560				}
561				seen[ap] = struct{}{}
562				endpoints = append(endpoints, ap)
563				specs = append(specs, EndpointSpec{Addr: ap})
564				continue
565			}
566			host, portStr, splitErr := net.SplitHostPort(s)
567			if splitErr != nil {
568				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, err)
569			}
570			// The host portion parses as an IP: this is a malformed IP-literal entry
571			// (e.g. a bad port), not a hostname — report the ORIGINAL ParseAddrPort
572			// error rather than diverting it into the hostname path, so every
573			// IP-shaped entry keeps today's exact error.
574			if _, ipErr := netip.ParseAddr(host); ipErr == nil {
575				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, err)
576			}
577			if !p.DNS {
578				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: hostname endpoints require the peer's dns = true opt-in flag", s)
579			}
580			port, portErr := strconv.ParseUint(portStr, 10, 16)
581			if portErr != nil || port == 0 {
582				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: invalid port %q", s, portStr)
583			}
584			if hostErr := validateHostname(host); hostErr != nil {
585				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, hostErr)
586			}
587			nameKey := host + ":" + portStr
588			if _, dup := seenNames[nameKey]; dup {
589				return fmt.Errorf("duplicate endpoint %q", s)
590			}
591			seenNames[nameKey] = struct{}{}
592			specs = append(specs, EndpointSpec{Host: host, Port: uint16(port), IsName: true})
593		}
594		p.Endpoints = endpoints
595		p.EndpointSpecs = specs
596		return nil
597	}
598	
599	// validateHostname reports whether host is a syntactically valid DNS hostname
600	// (RFC 1123 label rules): 1-253 characters total, each dot-separated label
601	// 1-63 characters of letters/digits/hyphens, no leading or trailing hyphen.
602	func validateHostname(host string) error {
603		if len(host) == 0 || len(host) > 253 {
604			return fmt.Errorf("hostname %q must be 1-253 characters", host)
605		}
606		for _, label := range strings.Split(host, ".") {
607			if len(label) == 0 || len(label) > 63 {
608				return fmt.Errorf("hostname label %q must be 1-63 characters", label)
609			}
610			if label[0] == '-' || label[len(label)-1] == '-' {
611				return fmt.Errorf("hostname label %q must not start or end with a hyphen", label)
612			}
613			for _, r := range label {
614				alnum := (r >= 'a' && r <= 'z') || (r >= 'A' && r <= 'Z') || (r >= '0' && r <= '9')
615				if !alnum && r != '-' {
616					return fmt.Errorf("hostname label %q contains invalid character %q", label, r)
617				}
618			}
619		}
620		return nil
621	}

623	// PeerIdentity is one configured WireGuard peer's effective outer-control PSK
624	// and stable name/id (G4 multi-peer concentrator groundwork).
625	type PeerIdentity struct {
626		// PSK is this peer's effective outer-control PSK: for a single-peer config
627		// it is the top-level Config.PSK (the pre-G4 back-compat default, unaffected
628		// by any per-peer psk); for a multi-peer config it is the peer's own PSK
629		// field. validate() rejects a per-peer psk when exactly one peer is
630		// configured, so the single-peer-with-distinct-per-peer-psk shape is not
631		// loadable via config.Load; this field still defends the invariant
632		// defensively for any Config value built directly.
633		PSK Key
634		// Name is this peer's stable identifier: its configured Name when set,
635		// otherwise a fallback derived from its public key (the first 8 bytes,
636		// lowercase hex — the same short-key form uapiConfig uses to name a peer
637		// in error messages), so every peer has a non-empty, stable id even when
638		// name is omitted.
639		Name string
640	}
641	
642	// PeerIdentities returns, for each configured WireGuard peer in order, its
643	// effective PSK and stable name/id (G4). This is the SINGLE place the
644	// single-peer/multi-peer PSK back-compat decision is made: device.Up and the
645	// Bind consume PeerIdentities instead of each re-deriving the effective PSK
646	// from Config.PSK vs Peer.PSK. Order matches c.WireGuard.Peers.
647	func (c Config) PeerIdentities() []PeerIdentity {
648		peers := c.WireGuard.Peers
649		ids := make([]PeerIdentity, len(peers))
650		for i, p := range peers {
651			psk := p.PSK
652			if len(peers) == 1 {
653				// Single-peer back-compat: the top-level psk remains the effective
654				// PSK regardless of whether a per-peer psk is also set.
655				psk = c.PSK
656			}
657			name := p.Name
658			if name == "" {
659				pub := p.PublicKey.Bytes()
660				name = hex.EncodeToString(pub[:8])
661			}
662			ids[i] = PeerIdentity{PSK: psk, Name: name}
663		}
664		return ids
665	}

667	// Amnezia holds the amneziawg-go obfuscation parameters. They must match on both
668	// ends for the handshake to succeed; they are defense-in-depth only.
669	//
670	// The block is "all-or-nothing": either every junk/size knob is left zero (plain
671	// WireGuard) or the whole obfuscation set (jc, jmin, jmax, s1, s2) is specified.
672	// A PARTIAL block silently produces an obfuscation profile the two ends cannot
673	// agree on, so validate rejects it at load (defect D1). The four magic headers
674	// (h1-h4) default to the standard message-type values 1..4 when omitted.
675	type Amnezia struct {
676		Jc   int    `toml:"jc"`
677		Jmin int    `toml:"jmin"`
678		Jmax int    `toml:"jmax"`
679		S1   int    `toml:"s1"`
680		S2   int    `toml:"s2"`
681		H1   uint32 `toml:"h1"`
682		H2   uint32 `toml:"h2"`
683		H3   uint32 `toml:"h3"`
684		H4   uint32 `toml:"h4"`
685	}
686	
687	// defaultMagicHeaders are the standard WireGuard message-type headers: initiation,
688	// response, cookie-reply, and transport. amneziawg-go treats any magic header <= 4
689	// as "use the standard type", so 1..4 is the canonical "headers not obfuscated"
690	// profile. wanbond emits them explicitly (rather than 0) so a configured amnezia
691	// block always carries a complete, self-consistent set of magic headers.
692	var defaultMagicHeaders = [4]uint32{1, 2, 3, 4}
693	
694	// Configured reports whether the amnezia block carries any obfuscation parameter.
695	// An all-zero block leaves the engine in plain WireGuard mode.
696	func (a Amnezia) Configured() bool {
697		return a.Jc != 0 || a.Jmin != 0 || a.Jmax != 0 || a.S1 != 0 || a.S2 != 0 ||
698			a.H1 != 0 || a.H2 != 0 || a.H3 != 0 || a.H4 != 0
699	}
700	
701	// applyDefaults fills in the standard magic headers (1..4) when the block is
702	// configured but no header was given, so the UAPI renderer emits an explicit,
703	// complete header set instead of h1=0..h4=0. It is a no-op for an unconfigured
704	// block and for one that already sets any header (a partial header set is left
705	// intact so validate can reject it).
706	func (a *Amnezia) applyDefaults() {
707		if !a.Configured() {
708			return
709		}
710		if a.H1 == 0 && a.H2 == 0 && a.H3 == 0 && a.H4 == 0 {
711			a.H1, a.H2, a.H3, a.H4 = defaultMagicHeaders[0], defaultMagicHeaders[1], defaultMagicHeaders[2], defaultMagicHeaders[3]
712		}
713	}

726	// Key is a 32-byte Curve25519 key or PSK, carried in TOML as standard base64.
727	type Key struct {
728		bytes [keyLen]byte
729		set   bool
730	}
731	
732	// UnmarshalText decodes a base64 key. An empty string leaves the Key unset so
733	// optional keys can be distinguished from present-but-invalid ones.
734	func (k *Key) UnmarshalText(text []byte) error {
735		if len(text) == 0 {
736			return nil
737		}
738		raw, err := base64.StdEncoding.DecodeString(string(text))
739		if err != nil {
740			return fmt.Errorf("key is not valid base64: %w", err)
741		}
742		if len(raw) != keyLen {
743			return fmt.Errorf("key must decode to %d bytes, got %d", keyLen, len(raw))
744		}
745		copy(k.bytes[:], raw)
746		k.set = true
747		return nil
748	}
749	
750	// IsSet reports whether the key was present and valid.
751	func (k Key) IsSet() bool { return k.set }
752	
753	// Bytes returns the raw key material.
754	func (k Key) Bytes() [keyLen]byte { return k.bytes }

756	// normalize parses the string-typed fields (addresses) into their typed forms.
757	func (c *Config) normalize() error {
758		for i := range c.Paths {
759			p := &c.Paths[i]
760			if p.SourceAddrRaw != "" {
761				addr, err := netip.ParseAddr(p.SourceAddrRaw)
762				if err != nil {
763					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid source_addr %q: %w", p.Name, p.SourceAddrRaw, err)
764				}
765				p.SourceAddr = addr
766			}
767			if p.DestAddrRaw != "" {
768				dst, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(p.DestAddrRaw)
769				if err != nil {
770					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid dest_addr %q: %w", p.Name, p.DestAddrRaw, err)
771				}
772				p.DestAddr = dst
773			}
774			if p.LinkBandwidthRaw != "" {
775				bw, err := parseBandwidth(p.LinkBandwidthRaw)
776				if err != nil {
777					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid link_bandwidth %q: %w", p.Name, p.LinkBandwidthRaw, err)
778				}
779				if bw <= 0 {
780					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: link_bandwidth must be > 0, got %q", p.Name, p.LinkBandwidthRaw)
781				}
782				p.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec = bw
783			}
784			if p.LinkRTTRaw != "" {
785				rtt, err := time.ParseDuration(p.LinkRTTRaw)
786				if err != nil {
787					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid link_rtt %q: %w", p.Name, p.LinkRTTRaw, err)
788				}
789				if rtt <= 0 {
790					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: link_rtt must be > 0, got %q", p.Name, p.LinkRTTRaw)
791				}
792				p.LinkRTT = rtt
793			}
794		}
795		for i := range c.WireGuard.Peers {
796			if err := c.WireGuard.Peers[i].resolveEndpoints(); err != nil {
797				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: %w", i, err)
798			}
799		}
800		c.Amnezia.applyDefaults()
801		// Derive the weighted pace from any operator-declared per-link bandwidth BEFORE
802		// applyDefaults, so it can (a) see the raw zero PerPathCapacityFPS to distinguish an
803		// explicit knob from the default and (b) set the derived capacity/burst that
804		// applyDefaults then leaves intact (it only fills a knob left at zero). Under a
805		// disabled or non-weighted pacing it is a no-op, so the synthetic default is kept.
806		if err := c.deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP(); err != nil {
807			return err
808		}
809		c.Scheduler.applyDefaults()
810		c.FEC.applyDefaults()
811		if err := c.DNS.applyDefaults(); err != nil {
812			return err
813		}
814		return nil
815	}
816	
817	// deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP sizes the weighted scheduler's per-path pace from the
818	// operator-declared per-link bandwidth (T53, Q20) via SizePacingFromBDP instead of the
819	// synthetic defaultPerPathCapacityFPS. It runs only under the weighted policy with
820	// pacing ENABLED and at least one declared link_bandwidth; otherwise a declared
821	// bandwidth is inert (pacing ships DISABLED by default), so an unrelated config is
822	// untouched. The value is OPERATOR-DECLARED and fixed at load — NOT runtime auto-tuning
823	// (Q20 rejected a live control loop for the pilot).
824	//
825	// The scheduler carries a SINGLE reference per-path capacity applied to every path's
826	// token bucket, so a heterogeneous link set is sized to the BOTTLENECK: the slowest
827	// declared link governs the shared pace, because pacing any path faster than the
828	// slowest link's capacity would let that link build the very standing queue pacing
829	// exists to prevent. All paths must therefore declare a bandwidth (all-or-nothing) so
830	// the bottleneck is well-defined across the whole path set.
831	func (c *Config) deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP() error {
832		s := &c.Scheduler
833		if s.Policy != PolicyWeighted || !s.PacingEnabled {
834			return nil
835		}
836		declared := 0
837		for i := range c.Paths {
838			if c.Paths[i].LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec > 0 {
839				declared++
840			}
841		}
842		if declared == 0 {
843			return nil // no declared bandwidth: the synthetic default pace is preserved.
844		}
845		if declared != len(c.Paths) {
846			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler pacing: link_bandwidth must be declared on ALL paths or none (got %d of %d) — the shared per-path pace is sized to the slowest declared link, which is undefined with a partial declaration", declared, len(c.Paths))
847		}
848		if s.PerPathCapacityFPS != 0 || s.PacingBurstFrames != 0 {
849			return errors.New("scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps / pacing_burst_frames and per-path link_bandwidth are mutually exclusive: declare the link bandwidth (BDP-derived pace) OR set the raw frame-slot knobs, not both")
850		}
851		var bottleneck BDPSizing
852		for i := range c.Paths {
853			p := &c.Paths[i]
854			if p.LinkRTT <= 0 {
855				return fmt.Errorf("path %q: link_rtt is required (> 0) when link_bandwidth is set under weighted pacing — it is the delay term of the bandwidth-delay-product burst", p.Name)
856			}
857			sz, err := SizePacingFromBDP(p.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec, p.LinkRTT, defaultAvgWireFrameBytes)
858			if err != nil {
859				return fmt.Errorf("path %q: %w", p.Name, err)
860			}
861			if i == 0 || sz.CapacityFPS < bottleneck.CapacityFPS {
862				bottleneck = sz
863			}
864		}
865		s.PerPathCapacityFPS = bottleneck.CapacityFPS
866		s.PacingBurstFrames = bottleneck.BurstFrames
867		return nil
868	}
869	
870	// applyDefaults selects active-backup when no policy is given and, only under the
871	// weighted policy, fills any weighted knob left at its zero value with its default.
872	// It is a no-op for the active-backup policy: those weighted knobs are inert there,
873	// so leaving them zero keeps the config surface for a P1 deployment empty.
874	func (s *SchedulerConfig) applyDefaults() {
875		if s.Policy == "" {
876			s.Policy = PolicyActiveBackup
877		}
878		if s.Policy != PolicyWeighted {
879			return
880		}
881		if s.PerPathCapacityFPS == 0 {
882			s.PerPathCapacityFPS = defaultPerPathCapacityFPS
883		}
884		if s.EngageFraction == 0 {
885			s.EngageFraction = defaultEngageFraction
886		}
887		if s.DisengageFraction == 0 {
888			s.DisengageFraction = defaultDisengageFraction
889		}
890		if s.CollapseDwell == 0 {
891			s.CollapseDwell = defaultCollapseDwell
892		}
893		if s.LoadTau == 0 {
894			s.LoadTau = defaultLoadTau
895		}
896		if s.PacingBurstFrames == 0 {
897			s.PacingBurstFrames = defaultPacingBurstFrames
898		}
899		if s.WeightRTTFloor == 0 {
900			s.WeightRTTFloor = defaultWeightRTTFloor
901		}
902		if s.WeightLossFloor == 0 {
903			s.WeightLossFloor = defaultWeightLossFloor
904		}
905	}
906	
907	// validate enforces the scheduler policy invariants. active-backup needs no tuning.
908	// The weighted policy fails fast on any out-of-range knob so a mis-tuned
909	// aggregation policy is rejected at load rather than misbehaving at runtime (the
910	// hysteresis band, in particular, is only a band when disengage < engage).
911	func (s SchedulerConfig) validate() error {
912		if !s.Policy.valid() {
913			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.policy must be %q or %q, got %q", PolicyActiveBackup, PolicyWeighted, s.Policy)
914		}
915		if s.Policy != PolicyWeighted {
916			return nil
917		}
918		if s.PerPathCapacityFPS <= 0 {
919			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps must be > 0 under the weighted policy, got %g", s.PerPathCapacityFPS)
920		}
921		if s.EngageFraction <= 0 || s.EngageFraction > 1 {
922			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.engage_fraction must be in (0,1], got %g", s.EngageFraction)
923		}
924		if s.DisengageFraction < 0 || s.DisengageFraction >= s.EngageFraction {
925			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.disengage_fraction must be in [0,engage_fraction=%g) to form a hysteresis band, got %g", s.EngageFraction, s.DisengageFraction)
926		}
927		if s.CollapseDwell < 0 {
928			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.collapse_dwell must be >= 0, got %s", s.CollapseDwell)
929		}
930		if s.LoadTau <= 0 {
931			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.load_tau must be > 0, got %s", s.LoadTau)
932		}
933		if s.PacingEnabled && s.PacingBurstFrames <= 0 {
934			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.pacing_burst_frames must be > 0 when pacing is enabled, got %g", s.PacingBurstFrames)
935		}
936		if s.WeightRTTFloor <= 0 {
937			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.weight_rtt_floor must be > 0 under the weighted policy, got %s", s.WeightRTTFloor)
938		}

950		if len(c.Paths) == 0 {
951			return errors.New("at least one path is required")
952		}
953		seen := make(map[string]struct{}, len(c.Paths))
954		// seenSrc maps an already-claimed source_addr to the path that claimed it, so a
955		// second path reusing it can be rejected at LOAD naming both conflicting paths.
956		// The multipath bind Opens each path on (source_addr, listen_port), so two paths
957		// sharing a source_addr collide EADDRINUSE at the second ListenUDP (and on every
958		// re-Open after Down/Up, since the engine passes the fixed port back) — a
959		// misconfiguration that must fail fast at load, not at bring-up (defect D10).

973			// (Is4In6) are distinct under netip == yet bind the identical v4 socket, so
974			// the two spellings must collide in the guard exactly as they do at bind.

983		}

985			return errors.New("at least one wireguard peer is required")
986		}
987		// The edge dials exactly one concentrator peer per process (Q21); multi-peer
988		// configs are concentrator-only scope. Check this before the per-peer loop
989		// below so an edge config with >1 peer is rejected with this scope-explaining
990		// message regardless of whether the peers are otherwise well-formed.
991		if c.Role == RoleEdge && len(c.WireGuard.Peers) > 1 {
992			return fmt.Errorf("edge role supports exactly one wireguard peer per process (got %d); the edge dials a single concentrator peer, multi-peer configs are concentrator-only", len(c.WireGuard.Peers))
993		}
994		for i, peer := range c.WireGuard.Peers {
995			if !peer.PublicKey.IsSet() {
996				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: public_key is required", i)
997			}

999			// a hostname-only peer contributes nothing to Endpoints at load time (Q30 —
1000			// resolution is deferred to runtime) but must still count as configured.
1001			if c.Role == RoleEdge && len(peer.EndpointSpecs) == 0 {
1002				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: endpoint is required for the edge role", i)
1003			}

1005			// NAT rebinds); an ordered failover endpoint list is meaningless for it, so
1006			// reject rather than silently ignore a mis-targeted config (Q18 is edge-side
1007			// only).

1009				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: endpoint/endpoints is not meaningful for the concentrator role (it learns the edge's endpoint dynamically)", i)
1010			}
1011			// The DNS opt-in (Q29) is edge-only, mirroring the endpoints rule above: a
1012			// concentrator learns the edge's endpoint dynamically, so a hostname
1013			// endpoint config (and its dns = true opt-in) is meaningless there even

1021		// psk is redundant, so reject one if given. With more than one peer, the

1043				}
1044				if prev, dup := seenPSKs[peer.PSK.Bytes()]; dup {

1055		}

1057			return err
1058		}
1059		if err := c.Scheduler.validate(); err != nil {
1060			return err
1061		}
1062		if err := c.FEC.validate(); err != nil {
1063			return err
1064		}
1065		if err := c.DNS.validate(); err != nil {

1071	// validate enforces the amnezia obfuscation invariants (defect D1). An
1072	// unconfigured block is valid (plain WireGuard). A configured block must specify
1073	// the WHOLE junk/size set and carry a consistent magic-header set, so a partial
1074	// or inconsistent obfuscation profile FAILS FAST at load rather than producing a
1075	// silently mismatched tunnel that never handshakes.
1076	//
1077	// This runs after applyDefaults, so an omitted magic-header set has already been
1078	// filled with the standard 1..4 values; the header check below therefore only
1079	// fires for a genuinely partial header set (some given, some left zero).
1080	func (a Amnezia) validate() error {
1081		if !a.Configured() {
1082			return nil
1083		}
1084		// All-or-nothing: enabling amnezia at all requires the full junk/size set, so
1085		// both ends are forced to specify the same complete profile. A partial block
1086		// (e.g. jc/jmin/jmax set but s1/s2 omitted) would leave the ends deriving
1087		// different profiles and the handshake would fail closed with no diagnostic.
1088		if a.Jc <= 0 || a.Jmin <= 0 || a.Jmax <= 0 || a.S1 <= 0 || a.S2 <= 0 {
1089			return fmt.Errorf("amnezia: incomplete obfuscation set — when configured, jc, jmin, jmax, s1 and s2 must all be > 0 (got jc=%d jmin=%d jmax=%d s1=%d s2=%d)",
1090				a.Jc, a.Jmin, a.Jmax, a.S1, a.S2)
1091		}
1092		if a.Jmin > a.Jmax {
1093			return fmt.Errorf("amnezia: require jmin <= jmax, got jmin=%d jmax=%d", a.Jmin, a.Jmax)
1094		}
1095		// Junk sizes must not collide: amneziawg-go classifies an incoming datagram's
1096		// message type by its on-the-wire length, so the obfuscated initiation packet
1097		// (MessageInitiationSize + s1) and response packet (MessageResponseSize + s2)
1098		// must differ in length. A colliding s1/s2 pair passes config load but is
1099		// rejected later by the engine's IpcSet ("new init size == new response size");
1100		// reject it here so the collision fails fast at load, at the right locus. The
1101		// engine's own MessageInitiationSize (148) and MessageResponseSize (92)
1102		// constants are used so this check tracks the vendored fork if it ever changes.
1103		if awgdevice.MessageInitiationSize+a.S1 == awgdevice.MessageResponseSize+a.S2 {
1104			return fmt.Errorf("amnezia: junk sizes collide — obfuscated init size %d (%d+s1) must differ from response size %d (%d+s2); adjust s1/s2",
1105				awgdevice.MessageInitiationSize+a.S1, awgdevice.MessageInitiationSize,
1106				awgdevice.MessageResponseSize+a.S2, awgdevice.MessageResponseSize)
1107		}
1108		// Magic headers must be distinct: the receive path classifies a datagram's
1109		// message type by its header value, so two equal headers make two message
1110		// types indistinguishable. An all-zero header set is left for applyDefaults;
1111		// any non-zero header requires a complete, distinct set (a partial set leaves
1112		// zeros here and is caught as a duplicate).
1113		if a.H1 != 0 || a.H2 != 0 || a.H3 != 0 || a.H4 != 0 {
1114			if a.H1 == a.H2 || a.H1 == a.H3 || a.H1 == a.H4 ||
1115				a.H2 == a.H3 || a.H2 == a.H4 || a.H3 == a.H4 {
1116				return fmt.Errorf("amnezia: magic headers must be a complete, distinct set, got h1=%d h2=%d h3=%d h4=%d",
1117					a.H1, a.H2, a.H3, a.H4)
1118			}
1119		}
1120		return nil
1121	}

13	// testKey returns a syntactically valid base64 32-byte key for fixtures.
14	func testKey(b byte) string {
15		raw := make([]byte, keyLen)
16		for i := range raw {
17			raw[i] = b
18		}
19		return base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(raw)

22	// writeConfig writes body to a temp file with the given mode and returns its path.
23	func writeConfig(t *testing.T, mode os.FileMode, body string) string {
24		t.Helper()
25		dir := t.TempDir()
26		path := filepath.Join(dir, "wanbond.toml")
27		if err := os.WriteFile(path, []byte(body), mode); err != nil {
28			t.Fatalf("write fixture: %v", err)
29		}
30		// os.WriteFile is subject to umask; force the exact bits.
31		if err := os.Chmod(path, mode); err != nil {
32			t.Fatalf("chmod fixture: %v", err)
33		}
34		return path
35	}

43	name = "starlink"

52	[[wireguard.peers]]
53	public_key = "%PUB%"
54	endpoint = "203.0.113.5:51820"
55	allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
56	
57	[amnezia]
58	jc = 4
59	jmin = 8
60	jmax = 80
61	s1 = 15
62	s2 = 92
63	
64	[metrics]

82	[[wireguard.peers]]
83	public_key = "%PUB%"
84	allowed_ips = ["10.0.0.2/32"]
85	`

87	func fill(tmpl string) string {
88		r := strings.NewReplacer("%PRIV%", testKey(1), "%PUB%", testKey(2), "%PSK%", testKey(3))
89		return r.Replace(tmpl)
90	}

92	func TestLoadValidEdge(t *testing.T) {
93		path := writeConfig(t, 0o600, fill(edgeConfig))
94		c, err := Load(path)
95		if err != nil {
96			t.Fatalf("Load: %v", err)
97		}
98		if c.Role != RoleEdge {
99			t.Errorf("role = %q, want edge", c.Role)
100		}
101		if len(c.Paths) != 2 {
102			t.Fatalf("paths = %d, want 2", len(c.Paths))
103		}
104		if got := c.Paths[0].SourceAddr.String(); got != "192.0.2.10" {
105			t.Errorf("path0 source_addr = %q, want 192.0.2.10", got)
106		}
107		if !c.WireGuard.PrivateKey.IsSet() || !c.PSK.IsSet() {
108			t.Error("private_key and psk must be set")
109		}
110		if c.Amnezia.Jmax != 80 {
111			t.Errorf("amnezia jmax = %d, want 80", c.Amnezia.Jmax)
112		}
113		// The fixture omits h1-h4, so Load must default them to the standard 1..4
114		// message-type headers (defect D1), never leave them at 0.
115		if got := [4]uint32{c.Amnezia.H1, c.Amnezia.H2, c.Amnezia.H3, c.Amnezia.H4}; got != [4]uint32{1, 2, 3, 4} {
116			t.Errorf("amnezia magic headers = %v, want defaulted [1 2 3 4]", got)
117		}
118	}

643	func TestLoadRejects(t *testing.T) {
644		cases := []struct {
645			name string
646			mode os.FileMode
647			body string
648			want string
649		}{
650			{
651				name: "insecure mode",
652				mode: 0o644,
653				body: fill(edgeConfig),
654				want: "insecure permissions",
655			},
656			{
657				name: "missing role",
658				mode: 0o600,
659				body: fill(strings.Replace(edgeConfig, `role = "edge"`, "", 1)),
660				want: "role must be",
661			},
662			{
663				name: "unknown role",
664				mode: 0o600,
665				body: fill(strings.Replace(edgeConfig, `role = "edge"`, `role = "relay"`, 1)),
666				want: "role must be",
667			},
668			{
669				name: "empty paths",
670				mode: 0o600,
671				body: fill(concentratorConfig[:strings.Index(concentratorConfig, "[[paths]]")] +
672					concentratorConfig[strings.Index(concentratorConfig, "[wireguard]"):]),
673				want: "at least one path",
674			},
675			{
676				name: "malformed key",
677				mode: 0o600,
678				body: fill(strings.Replace(edgeConfig, "%PRIV%", "not-base64!!", 1)),
679				want: "base64",
680			},
681			{
682				name: "wrong key length",
683				mode: 0o600,
684				body: fill(strings.Replace(edgeConfig, "%PRIV%", base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString([]byte("short")), 1)),
685				want: "decode to 32 bytes",
686			},
687			{
688				name: "edge peer without endpoint",
689				mode: 0o600,
690				body: fill(strings.Replace(edgeConfig, `endpoint = "203.0.113.5:51820"`, "", 1)),
691				want: "endpoint is required",
692			},
693			{
694				name: "edge peer endpoint and endpoints both set",
695				mode: 0o600,
696				body: fill(strings.Replace(edgeConfig, `endpoint = "203.0.113.5:51820"`,
697					"endpoint = \"203.0.113.5:51820\"\nendpoints = [\"198.51.100.1:51820\"]", 1)),
698				want: "mutually exclusive",
699			},
700			{
701				name: "edge peer endpoints unparseable entry",
702				mode: 0o600,
703				body: fill(strings.Replace(edgeConfig, `endpoint = "203.0.113.5:51820"`,
704					`endpoints = ["203.0.113.5:51820", "not-a-host-port"]`, 1)),
705				want: "invalid endpoint",
706			},
707			{
708				name: "edge peer endpoints duplicate entry",
709				mode: 0o600,
710				body: fill(strings.Replace(edgeConfig, `endpoint = "203.0.113.5:51820"`,
711					`endpoints = ["203.0.113.5:51820", "203.0.113.5:51820"]`, 1)),
712				want: "duplicate endpoint",
713			},
714			{
715				name: "edge peer endpoints empty list",
716				mode: 0o600,
717				body: fill(strings.Replace(edgeConfig, `endpoint = "203.0.113.5:51820"`,
718					`endpoints = []`, 1)),
719				want: "endpoint is required",
720			},
721			{
722				name: "concentrator peer declares endpoints (edge-only surface)",
723				mode: 0o600,
724				body: fill(strings.Replace(concentratorConfig, `allowed_ips = ["10.0.0.2/32"]`,
725					"allowed_ips = [\"10.0.0.2/32\"]\nendpoints = [\"203.0.113.5:51820\"]", 1)),
726				want: "not meaningful for the concentrator role",
727			},
728			{
729				// Acceptance case (2): a hostname entry without the peer's dns = true
730				// opt-in fails Load with an error naming the flag (Q29 default-off
731				// DPI posture).
732				name: "edge peer hostname endpoint without dns opt-in",
733				mode: 0o600,
734				body: fill(strings.Replace(edgeConfig, `endpoint = "203.0.113.5:51820"`,
735					`endpoint = "concentrator.example.com:51820"`, 1)),
736				want: "dns = true",
737			},
738			{
739				// Acceptance case (4): duplicate detection extends to hostname entries
740				// — the same host:port twice is rejected, same error shape as a
741				// duplicate IP literal.
742				name: "edge peer duplicate hostname endpoint",
743				mode: 0o600,
744				body: fill(strings.Replace(edgeConfig, `endpoint = "203.0.113.5:51820"`,
745					"dns = true\n"+`endpoints = ["concentrator.example.com:51820", "concentrator.example.com:51820"]`, 1)),
746				want: "duplicate endpoint",
747			},
748			{
749				// Acceptance case (5): the dns opt-in is edge-only, mirroring the
750				// endpoints-not-meaningful-for-concentrator rule, even when the
751				// concentrator declares no endpoint/endpoints entries at all.
752				name: "concentrator peer declares dns opt-in (edge-only surface)",
753				mode: 0o600,
754				body: fill(strings.Replace(concentratorConfig, `allowed_ips = ["10.0.0.2/32"]`,
755					"allowed_ips = [\"10.0.0.2/32\"]\ndns = true", 1)),
756				want: "not meaningful for the concentrator role",
757			},
758			{
759				name: "concentrator without listen_port",
760				mode: 0o600,
761				body: fill(strings.Replace(concentratorConfig, "listen_port = 51820", "", 1)),
762				want: "listen_port is required",
763			},
764			{
765				name: "missing psk",
766				mode: 0o600,
767				body: fill(strings.Replace(edgeConfig, "psk = \"%PSK%\"", "", 1)),
768				want: "psk is required",
769			},
770			{
771				name: "fec enabled without data_shards",
772				mode: 0o600,
773				body: fill(edgeConfig) + "\n[fec]\nenabled = true\nparity_shards = 2\n",
774				want: "fec.data_shards must be >= 1",
775			},
776			{
777				name: "fec enabled without parity_shards",
778				mode: 0o600,
779				body: fill(edgeConfig) + "\n[fec]\nenabled = true\ndata_shards = 8\n",
780				want: "fec.parity_shards must be >= 1",
781			},
782			{
783				name: "fec ratio exceeds field limit",
784				mode: 0o600,
785				body: fill(edgeConfig) + "\n[fec]\nenabled = true\ndata_shards = 250\nparity_shards = 10\n",
786				want: "Reed-Solomon field limit",
787			},
788			{
789				name: "fec deadline exceeds resequencer budget",
790				mode: 0o600,
791				// go-toml/v2 decodes a duration as integer nanoseconds; 500ms = 5e8 ns > 125ms.
792				body: fill(edgeConfig) + "\n[fec]\nenabled = true\ndata_shards = 8\nparity_shards = 3\ndeadline = 500000000\n",
793				want: "fec.deadline must be <=",
794			},
795			{
796				name: "fec adaptive without enabled",
797				mode: 0o600,
798				body: fill(edgeConfig) + "\n[fec]\nadaptive = true\n",
799				want: "fec.adaptive = true requires fec.enabled = true",
800			},
801			{
802				name: "fec target_residual out of range (>= 1)",
803				mode: 0o600,
804				body: fill(edgeConfig) + "\n[fec]\nenabled = true\nadaptive = true\ndata_shards = 10\nparity_shards = 6\ntarget_residual = 1.5\n",
805				want: "fec.target_residual must be a finite value in (0,1)",
806			},

811				want: "fec.target_residual must be a finite value in (0,1)",

846	// TestLoadRejectsDuplicateSourceAddr is the D10 regression. validate() enforced
847	// unique path NAMES but not unique source_addr values; the multipath bind Opens
848	// each path on (source_addr, port), so two paths sharing a source_addr collide
